explanation: The phrase "When prop_0, prop_1" means that whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must also be true at that same point in time. This condition must hold true globally (at all times). Therefore, it translates to a global implication.

The natural language phrase 'When prop_0, prop_1' implies that whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must also be true at that same instant. This is a universal implication, meaning it must hold true at all points in time. Therefore, the 'Globally' (G) operator is used to assert that the implication 'prop_0 implies prop_1' is always true.

The phrase "When prop_0, prop_1" typically translates to a global implication where the occurrence of 'prop_0' at any point necessitates the eventual occurrence of 'prop_1'. This is expressed as 'G (prop_0 -> F prop_1)', meaning "Globally, if prop_0 is true, then eventually prop_1 must be true."
The phrase 'When prop_0, prop_1' implies that if prop_0 is true at any point, then prop_1 must also be true at that same point. This is a direct implication.

The phrase "When prop_0, prop_1" indicates that whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must also be true at that exact moment. This is a global implication, meaning it must hold true at all points in time. Therefore, the LTL translation is G(prop_0 -> prop_1), which reads as "Always, if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 is true."
The phrase 'When prop_0, prop_1' implies that whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must also be true at that same moment. This translates directly to a global implication: G(prop_0 -> prop_1).
The phrase "When prop_0, prop_1" means that if prop_0 is true at any point, then prop_1 must also be true at that same point in time. This is a direct implication.

The phrase 'When prop_0, prop_1' implies that if 'prop_0' is true at any point, then 'prop_1' must also be true at that same point. This is a global implication, meaning it must hold true at all times. Therefore, it translates to G(prop_0 -> prop_1).
The phrase "When prop_0, prop_1" indicates a global implication: whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must also be true at that same moment. This is directly translated to G(prop_0 -> prop_1).

The phrase 'When prop_0, prop_1' implies a global relationship where if 'prop_0' is true at any point in time, then 'prop_1' must also be true at that same point in time. This is best represented by a global implication.
